7.experiment_plan:
# ML Experimentation & Modelling Strategy
## Multi-Class NLP Classification of Pharmacy Intervention Documents

**Document Type:** Phase 6 Experiment Plan  
**Task:** Supervised 5-class text classification of free-text pharmacy intervention (PI) records  
**Author Role:** Principal ML Scientist / Healthcare AI Researcher  
**Date:** June 2026  
**Status:** Ready for code generation

> **Note:** Train, validation, and test sets have already been split. All validation procedures operate within the existing split structure. The test set is locked and must not be used for any model selection or hyperparameter tuning decision.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Recommended Model Families](#1-recommended-model-families)
2. [Prioritised Model List](#2-prioritised-model-list)
3. [Iterative Enhancement Strategy](#3-iterative-enhancement-strategy)
4. [Validation Framework](#4-validation-framework)
5. [Explainability Approach](#5-explainability-approach)
6. [Experiment Tracking Strategy](#6-experiment-tracking-strategy)
7. [Risk Mitigation](#7-risk-mitigation)
8. [Appendix: Evaluation Metrics Reference](#appendix-evaluation-metrics-reference)

---

## 1. Recommended Model Families

### 1.1 Domain-Adapted BERT Variants

**Models:** BioBERT v1.1, PubMedBERT, BioClinicalBERT, BioClinical ModernBERT

**Rationale & Suitability**  
BERT-family encoders are the de-facto standard for clinical text classification and are the primary recommendation for this task. Their pre-training on biomedical literature (PubMed, PMC) and/or clinical EHR notes (MIMIC-III) yields rich contextual representations of drug names, dosing language, and clinical concepts that general-domain models cannot match. The 5-class mutually-exclusive classification structure maps cleanly to a `[CLS]`-token softmax head fine-tuned end-to-end. Alkanj et al. (2025) directly validated this approach on an 11-category pharmacy DRP corpus, confirming technical feasibility and strong performance.

- **PubMedBERT** is trained from scratch on PubMed text (not adapted from general BERT), giving it the strongest vocabulary alignment for journal-style or structured clinical language.
- **BioClinicalBERT** bridges biomedical literature and EHR notes (MIMIC-III), making it optimal when PI documents contain informal free-text alongside pharmacological content.
- **BioClinical ModernBERT** (2025) adds an 8,192-token context window via RoPE, flash attention, and two-trillion-token pre-training — the current SOTA encoder for clinical NLP. It eliminates chunking artefacts for any PI records containing extended medication histories.

**Expected Strengths**
- Best in-class vocabulary coverage for drug names, dosing terms, and clinical abbreviations
- Proven macro-F1 of 85–93% on comparable 5-class clinical classification tasks
- Fast inference (~5–15ms per document on GPU); suitable for real-time point-of-submission classification
- Well-supported HuggingFace ecosystem; straightforward fine-tuning pipeline

**Expected Weaknesses**
- Requires ~200–500 labelled examples per class to fine-tune reliably; performance degrades sharply below this threshold
- Base BERT variants cap at 512 tokens; very long PI records require truncation or chunking (mitigated by ModernBERT)
- Risk of overfitting on small datasets without regularisation (LoRA, dropout, early stopping)

**Deployment Considerations**
- Model size: ~110M parameters (base); fits comfortably on a single GPU or CPU server for batch inference
- PHI/HIPAA: fully on-premise; no data leaves the hospital network — compliant by default
- Inference can be containerised (Docker/ONNX) for integration into pharmacy information systems
- Model updates require re-fine-tuning on new labelled data; schedule periodic retraining as formularies change

---

### 1.2 RoBERTa and Biomedical Variants

**Models:** RoBERTa-base/large, BioMed-RoBERTa, RoBERTa-large-PM-M3-Voken

**Rationale & Suitability**  
RoBERTa improves on BERT by using dynamic masking, larger batch sizes, longer training, and removing the Next Sentence Prediction objective — changes that consistently improve downstream F1 on classification tasks. Clinical NLP studies show BioMed-RoBERTa achieving higher F1 than base BioBERT on short-to-medium documents, which describes the majority of PI records. It serves as a strong secondary challenger to BioClinicalBERT.

**Expected Strengths**
- More robust pre-training than original BERT; less sensitive to hyperparameter choices
- BioMed-RoBERTa trained on PubMed + PMC gives strong biomedical vocabulary coverage
- Consistently competitive F1 (83–89%) across biomedical classification benchmarks

**Expected Weaknesses**
- Biomedical RoBERTa variants are less exposed to clinical EHR notes than BioClinicalBERT; may underperform on informal pharmacy free-text
- Same 512-token context limit as base BERT variants

**Deployment Considerations**
- Identical footprint and on-premise compliance as BERT variants
- Can be used interchangeably with BioClinicalBERT in the inference pipeline

---

### 1.3 Fine-Tuned Small LLMs

**Models:** Llama 3.1/3.2 (8B), Mistral-7B, Med42, BioMedLM

**Rationale & Suitability**  
Fine-tuned small LLMs (7–13B parameters) with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) have been shown (MedSlice 2025, Med42 2024) to be competitive with encoder-only BERT models on clinical text classification while additionally offering generative rationale alongside predictions — a valuable feature for pharmacist trust. They are particularly relevant when label data is limited or when the task requires capturing long-range contextual dependencies across complex PI documents.

**Expected Strengths**
- Can produce a natural-language justification alongside the class prediction, supporting clinical adoption
- Instruction-tuning paradigm enables adaptation with fewer labelled examples
- Stronger handling of complex, multi-sentence PI records with dense co-references

**Expected Weaknesses**
- 8–15× higher GPU memory requirement than BERT-class models (~16–40GB VRAM for 7–8B models)
- Inference latency 5–10× slower than encoders; may not support real-time point-of-submission classification without quantisation (INT8/INT4)
- Higher risk of hallucinated confidence — model may express certainty on incorrect predictions
- Requires careful SFT pipeline setup; more complex training than encoder fine-tuning

**Deployment Considerations**
- On-premise deployment feasible but requires dedicated GPU infrastructure (A100 or equivalent)
- Quantised versions (4-bit GPTQ/AWQ) can reduce memory to ~6–8GB with minor accuracy loss
- PHI/HIPAA compliant if self-hosted; do not use commercial API endpoints with PI documents
- Best suited as an offline batch classifier or a secondary human-review assistant rather than a real-time router

---

### 1.4 Large Language Models — Zero/Few-Shot

**Models:** GPT-4o, Claude 3.5/3.7, Gemini 1.5 Pro

**Rationale & Suitability**  
Zero/few-shot LLMs are not recommended for production classification in this setting, but are highly valuable as auxiliary tools. JAMIA (2024) showed GPT-4 zero-shot achieves higher recall but lower F1 than supervised PLMs. Primary use cases here are: (1) generating synthetic PI documents for minority classes with low support, (2) bootstrapping weak labels for unlabelled documents, and (3) generating chain-of-thought rationales to augment training data for encoder fine-tuning.

**Expected Strengths**
- No labelled training data required; useful for rapid prototyping and annotation bootstrapping
- High recall — useful for identifying rare intervention types that supervised models miss
- GPT-4-annotated data has been shown (Zong et al., JAMIA 2024) to produce superior fine-tuned encoders compared to GPT-3.5-annotated data

**Expected Weaknesses**
- Lower macro-F1 than fine-tuned models (~60–72%); not suitable as a standalone classifier
- Non-deterministic outputs; results vary across runs without temperature=0 and fixed seeds
- API cost at scale; latency unsuitable for real-time deployment

**Deployment Considerations**
- **CRITICAL PHI/HIPAA risk:** Routing pharmacy intervention documents to any commercial API endpoint (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) constitutes a potential HIPAA violation unless a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) is in place and the endpoint is a HIPAA-eligible service. Consult your institution's data governance team before any use.
- Recommended use: offline annotation pipeline only, with de-identified documents if possible, never in the live clinical workflow

---

### 1.5 Classical ML with TF-IDF / Clinical Embeddings

**Models:** Logistic Regression + TF-IDF, Linear SVM + TF-IDF, XGBoost + FastText/Word2Vec

**Rationale & Suitability**  
Classical ML models are mandatory baselines and remain surprisingly competitive in low-data regimes (< 500 labelled examples per class). They are highly interpretable, fast to train, and easy to audit — properties that are valued in regulated clinical environments. Literature from JMIR (2025) and Ling et al. (2023) confirms these remain competitive for pharmaceutical NLP tasks with limited annotation budgets. TF-IDF + LinearSVC is frequently the strongest classical baseline for short-to-medium text.

**Expected Strengths**
- No GPU required; trains in seconds to minutes on CPU
- Coefficient weights provide direct, auditable feature attribution — pharmacists can inspect which terms drive each prediction
- Minimal overfitting risk; strong generalisation on small labelled datasets
- Best macro-F1 baseline achievable without any pre-training (~68–76%)

**Expected Weaknesses**
- No contextual understanding; identical drug names in different contexts receive the same representation
- Misses synonyms, paraphrases, and multi-word clinical phrases unless n-gram TF-IDF is used
- Performance ceiling well below domain-adapted transformers

**Deployment Considerations**
- Trivially deployable on any server; model file is kilobytes in size
- Fully on-premise; no infrastructure requirements
- Highly auditable for regulatory and clinical governance purposes — recommended as the fallback/explainability model even after a transformer is deployed

---

### 1.6 TextCNN over Clinical Word Embeddings

**Models:** TextCNN (Kim 2014), DPCNN

**Rationale & Suitability**  
TextCNN captures local n-gram patterns via parallel convolutional filters at multiple window sizes (e.g., 2, 3, 4 tokens). This is well-suited to pharmacy intervention text, where short, discriminative phrases like "dose adjustment recommended", "contraindicated in renal failure", or "therapeutic duplication identified" carry most of the classification signal. TextCNN serves as the strongest intermediate baseline between classical ML and transformers.

**Expected Strengths**
- Significantly faster to train than transformers; GPU optional
- Captures local n-gram patterns not captured by bag-of-words TF-IDF
- Expected macro-F1 of 72–80% — a meaningful step above classical ML

**Expected Weaknesses**
- No long-range dependency modelling; misses cross-sentence contextual information
- Less interpretable than logistic regression; requires LIME/SHAP for post-hoc explanation
- Inferior to BERT-class models in most benchmarks; primarily serves as an ablation data point

**Deployment Considerations**
- Lightweight; can run on CPU in production if GPU unavailable
- On-premise; no PHI risk

---

## 2. Prioritised Model List

Models are ordered by experimental priority — the sequence in which they should be trained, evaluated, and compared. Priority is determined by expected return-on-investment (performance gain per implementation effort), role in establishing robust baselines, and deployment feasibility.

| Priority | Model | Role | Expected Macro-F1 | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Majority-class classifier | Lower bound baseline | 15–25% | Mandatory floor; quantifies class imbalance severity |
| **P2** | TF-IDF + Logistic Regression | Interpretable baseline | 65–73% | Standard NLP baseline; fast; auditable; establishes classical ML ceiling |
| **P3** | TF-IDF + Linear SVM | Strong classical baseline | 68–76% | Frequently best classical text classifier; validates or challenges LR |
| **P4** | FastText / clinical Word2Vec + MLP | Embedding baseline | 68–76% | Tests subword embeddings; handles OOV drug names; ablation vs TF-IDF |
| **P5** | TextCNN + clinical embeddings | Neural n-gram baseline | 72–80% | First neural model; bridges classical ML and transformers |
| **P6** | BiLSTM + clinical FastText | Sequential neural baseline | 70–78% | Tests sequential modelling without attention; ablation vs TextCNN |
| **P7** | BERT-base (uncased) | General transformer baseline | 75–83% | Confirms that domain adaptation over general BERT provides measurable gain |
| **P8** | BioBERT v1.1 | Domain transformer baseline | 80–87% | Established biomedical baseline; well-benchmarked reference point |
| **P9** | GPT-4o zero-shot | LLM out-of-box reference | 60–72% | Quantifies prompt-only capability; informs synthetic data generation strategy |
| **P10** | BioClinicalBERT (fine-tuned) | **Primary model candidate** | 85–91% | Best balance of domain alignment, compute, and maturity; clinical EHR exposure |
| **P11** | PubMedBERT (fine-tuned) | Primary model candidate | 85–91% | Strong alternative if PI documents skew toward structured clinical language |
| **P12** | BioMed-RoBERTa (fine-tuned) | Challenger model | 83–89% | Tests whether RoBERTa pre-training advantages transfer to this domain |
| **P13** | BioClinical ModernBERT (fine-tuned, LoRA) | **SOTA candidate** | 88–93% | Current SOTA encoder for clinical NLP; recommended if compute allows |
| **P14** | BioClinicalBERT + class-weighted focal loss | Imbalance-aware variant | +2–4% over P10 | Ablation: tests focal loss vs weighted cross-entropy for minority classes |
| **P15** | BioClinicalBERT + LoRA adapters | Efficient fine-tuning variant | Comparable to P10 | Tests adapter efficiency; preferred path for continual/institutional re-training |
| **P16** | Fine-tuned Llama 3.1 8B (SFT) | Advanced generative model | 83–90% | Only if GPU infrastructure permits; evaluated for rationale generation |
| **P17** | GPT-4 pseudo-label + BioClinicalBERT | SOTA pipeline | 82–88% | If minority class support is insufficient; uses GPT-4 annotation offline |

**Priority ordering rationale:** P1–P6 establish the performance ladder from trivial to neural, ensuring any claimed gains from transformers are genuine. P7–P8 isolate the domain adaptation contribution. P9 provides a zero-shot LLM reference that informs whether synthetic data generation is warranted. P10–P13 are the primary competitive models. P14–P17 are enhancement experiments run only after a primary model is selected.

---

## 3. Iterative Enhancement Strategy

The experiment progression follows five stages. Each stage gate requires a decision before advancing.

---

### Stage 1 — Baseline Establishment (P1–P6)

**Objective:** Establish a robust performance ladder from trivial to neural baselines. All models evaluated on the validation set using macro-F1, macro ROC-AUC, micro ROC-AUC, and per-class precision/recall.

**Models:** Majority-class classifier → TF-IDF + LR → TF-IDF + LinearSVM → FastText + MLP → TextCNN → BiLSTM

**Expected outcome:** TF-IDF + LinearSVM achieves the best classical macro-F1 (~68–76%). TextCNN adds ~4–5 points via n-gram modelling.

**Stage gate:** If TextCNN validation macro-F1 < 60%, flag a potential data quality or class imbalance issue before proceeding. Investigate class confusion matrix and revisit pre-processing.

**Stopping criteria:** All six models trained and evaluated. Do not skip baselines — they are required for the final comparison table and for establishing the human-interpretable fallback model.

---

### Stage 2 — Transformer Baselines (P7–P9)

**Objective:** Confirm that domain-adapted pre-training provides a measurable improvement over general BERT. Quantify the LLM zero-shot ceiling.

**Models:** BERT-base (uncased) → BioBERT v1.1 → GPT-4o zero-shot

**Expected outcome:** BioBERT outperforms BERT-base by ~3–7 macro-F1 points, confirming domain adaptation is worthwhile. GPT-4 zero-shot provides a recall-high but F1-lower reference point.

**Stage gate:** If BioBERT does not outperform BERT-base by ≥2 macro-F1 points, consider whether the PI text is sufficiently close to biomedical literature (vs. informal clinical free-text), and adjust the primary model selection accordingly (favour BioClinicalBERT even more strongly).

---

### Stage 3 — Primary Model Selection (P10–P13)

**Objective:** Identify the best fine-tuned domain encoder for this specific dataset.

**Models:** BioClinicalBERT → PubMedBERT → BioMed-RoBERTa → BioClinical ModernBERT (LoRA)

**Fine-tuning protocol for each:**
- Optimizer: AdamW, lr=2e-5, weight_decay=0.01
- Epochs: up to 10 with early stopping (patience=3) on validation macro-F1
- Batch size: 16 (gradient accumulation to 32 if GPU memory limited)
- Class weighting: inverse-frequency weights applied to cross-entropy loss by default
- Max sequence length: 512 (base models) or 1024 (ModernBERT)
- Seeds: train each model with 3 different random seeds; report mean ± std

**Expected outcome:** BioClinicalBERT and PubMedBERT perform similarly (~85–91%). BioClinical ModernBERT achieves the best result (~88–93%) if compute permits.

**Stage gate:** Select the single best model by validation macro-F1 (breaking ties by macro ROC-AUC). This becomes the **primary model** for all Stage 4 enhancements. Do not run Stage 4 experiments on multiple base models simultaneously.

---

### Stage 4 — Enhancement Experiments (P14–P17)

Run each enhancement as an ablation against the Stage 3 primary model. Each experiment changes exactly one component.

**4a — Loss function:** Replace class-weighted cross-entropy with focal loss (α=class_weights, γ=2). Expected gain: +1–4 macro-F1 points on minority classes, especially if imbalance ratio > 5:1.

**4b — LoRA fine-tuning:** Replace full fine-tuning with LoRA adapters (r=16, α=32, target modules: query/value projections). Expected outcome: comparable F1 with 60–80% fewer trainable parameters; preferred path for future institutional re-training.

**4c — Text augmentation:** Apply back-translation (English → French → English via Helsinki-NLP models, offline) to minority class training examples. Augment to 1.5× original minority class size. Expected gain: +1–3 macro-F1 on minority classes.

**4d — GPT-4 pseudo-label pipeline (if needed):** Only activate if any class has < 100 training examples. Generate synthetic PI documents via offline GPT-4 prompting on de-identified templates; have one pharmacist review a 10% sample for clinical plausibility; add accepted synthetic examples to training set.

**Stage gate:** Adopt an enhancement if it improves validation macro-F1 by ≥0.5 points and does not degrade any individual class F1 by > 2 points. Enhancements can be stacked if independent.

---

### Stage 5 — Final Evaluation

**Objective:** Single locked evaluation on the held-out test set using the best model configuration from Stages 3–4.

- Report all stakeholder-mandated metrics: macro ROC-AUC, micro ROC-AUC, max F1
- Additionally report: macro-F1, per-class precision/recall/F1, confusion matrix, calibration plot (ECE), Cohen's κ
- Run inference with 3 seeds; report mean ± std on all metrics
- Apply temperature scaling to calibrate output probabilities before final evaluation
- Document all hyperparameters, software versions, and random seeds in the experiment tracker

**No further model changes are permitted after Stage 5 begins.**

---

## 4. Validation Framework

### 4.1 Cross-Validation Strategy

Apply **stratified 5-fold cross-validation on the training set** during hyperparameter search for classical ML models (Stages 1–2). This ensures each fold preserves the class distribution of the full training set.

For transformer fine-tuning (Stages 3–4), use the pre-split validation set as the tuning target (not k-fold CV) due to the computational cost of multiple fine-tuning runs. Instead, train each transformer with **3 random seeds** and report mean ± std to assess stability.

- **k:** 5 for classical ML; 3-seed repeat for transformers
- **Stratification:** Always stratify by `label`; never use random unstratified splits
- **Metric for fold aggregation:** Macro-F1 (mean across folds)

### 4.2 Holdout Strategy

The test set is **fully locked** from the start of experimentation. The following rules are non-negotiable:

- No model selection, hyperparameter tuning, or architecture decision may use test set performance
- The test set is evaluated exactly once, after Stage 4 is complete and the final model is frozen
- Any accidental test set evaluation must be logged and disclosed; that model configuration is disqualified from final selection
- Validation set is used for all model selection decisions (early stopping, hyperparameter tuning, enhancement adoption)

### 4.3 Hyperparameter Tuning

- **Classical ML:** Grid search over validation macro-F1. TF-IDF: `{ngram_range: (1,1),(1,2),(1,3)}, max_features: {10k, 30k, 50k}`. LR: `C: {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}`. SVM: `C: {0.1, 1, 10}`.
- **Transformers:** Manual search over `{lr: 1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5} × {batch_size: 16, 32}`. Use Optuna for automated search if > 3 transformer candidates are being compared simultaneously.
- **Tuning objective:** Validation macro-F1 in all cases. Do not tune on accuracy or loss.

### 4.4 Data Leakage Prevention

| Leakage Type | Risk in This Setting | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Patient-level leakage** | Same patient's PI records appearing in both train and test | If patient IDs are available, split by patient ID, not document ID. Verify no patient appears in both train and validation sets. |
| **Temporal leakage** | Training on future records to predict past ones | If timestamps are available, confirm train set predates validation set, which predates test set. If temporal ordering was used for the original split, document this. |
| **Annotator leakage** | Pharmacist writing style or idiosyncratic phrasing leaking class identity | Strip pharmacist identifiers from all documents before training. Analyse whether any single annotator dominates a single class. |
| **Pre-processing leakage** | Fitting TF-IDF vocabulary or scaler statistics on validation/test data | All `fit()` calls (TF-IDF, LabelEncoder, scaler) must be performed on training data only; `transform()` applied to validation and test. |
| **Validation set contamination** | Using validation performance to make dozens of decisions, causing implicit overfitting | Cap the number of hyperparameter configurations evaluated against the validation set. For transformers, use early stopping rather than exhaustive grid search. |

### 4.5 Statistical Significance

Before concluding that Model A outperforms Model B:

- Compute **McNemar's test** on the per-document predictions of the two models against the validation set labels (appropriate for paired classification comparison)
- Report **bootstrap 95% confidence intervals** (n=1000 bootstrap samples) around macro-F1 for all final reported metrics
- A difference is considered meaningful only if p < 0.05 on McNemar's test AND the 95% CI of the difference does not include zero
- For transformer models trained with multiple seeds, also report mean ± std macro-F1 across seeds

---

## 5. Explainability Approach

### 5.1 Inherently Interpretable Models (Classical ML)

**Logistic Regression + TF-IDF:**
- Extract top-20 positive and negative coefficient tokens per class
- Present as a ranked table: `{token, coefficient, class}` — directly auditable by pharmacists
- Generate a global feature importance bar chart per class for the experiment report

**Linear SVM:**
- Extract decision boundary weight vectors; same coefficient-ranking approach as LR
- For misclassified documents, report which tokens contributed most to the incorrect class boundary

**Presentation format for pharmacists:** A static PDF or dashboard table showing the top 10 words most predictive of each intervention class, with example documents. This requires no additional tooling and supports clinical governance review.

### 5.2 Post-Hoc Explanation for Transformer Models

**SHAP (primary tool):**
- Use `shap.Explainer` with the fine-tuned transformer and a background corpus sample (n=100 documents)
- Generate both local explanations (per-document token attribution for individual predictions) and global explanations (mean absolute SHAP values across the corpus per class)
- SHAP is preferred over LIME for theoretical consistency (Shapley values are the unique solution to the axioms of fair attribution) and for producing more stable explanations across runs
- Known limitation: SHAP on transformers is computationally expensive; use `shap.Partition Explainer` with token-level masking for efficiency

**LIME (secondary tool):**
- Apply for rapid auditing of individual high-stakes or borderline predictions during clinical review
- Computationally lighter than SHAP; generates local linear surrogates per document in seconds
- Known limitation: LIME explanations are unstable across runs due to random perturbation sampling; do not use LIME for global feature importance

**Integrated Gradients:**
- Apply via `captum` library on the fine-tuned transformer
- Provides gradient-based token attribution that is native to the model architecture and does not require masking perturbations
- Preferred for technical validation and debugging; less interpretable to non-ML pharmacist audiences than SHAP

**Attention weight visualisation:**
- Use `BertViz` for qualitative inspection during development
- Do not present attention weights as causal explanations to pharmacists; attention is not attribution — communicate this limitation clearly in any clinical documentation

### 5.3 Calibration

Calibrated probabilities are required for the confidence-threshold routing system (low-confidence predictions → human review queue).

**Method:** Apply **temperature scaling** post-training on the validation set. Temperature scaling fits a single scalar parameter T on validation set logits to minimise NLL, without changing the model weights or decision boundaries. It is the recommended calibration method for neural classifiers (Guo et al., 2017).

**Evaluation:** Plot a reliability diagram and report Expected Calibration Error (ECE) before and after temperature scaling for the final model.

**Threshold setting:** After calibration, set per-class confidence thresholds on the validation set to achieve ≥ 90% precision on auto-classified documents. Documents below threshold are routed to pharmacist review. Document the threshold values in the model card.

### 5.4 Clinical Presentation Format

Each model prediction surfaced to a pharmacist should include:

1. **Predicted class label** with the full intervention type description
2. **Confidence score** (calibrated probability, expressed as a percentage)
3. **Top 5 influential tokens** from SHAP local explanation, highlighted in the original document text
4. **Human review flag** if confidence < class-specific threshold
5. **Alternative class** if the second-highest probability is within 10 percentage points of the top prediction (potential ambiguity signal)

---

## 6. Experiment Tracking Strategy

### 6.1 Tracking Tool

**Recommended:** MLflow (self-hosted on-premise)

**Rationale:** MLflow is open-source, fully self-hosted (no PHI leaves the hospital network), integrates natively with PyTorch/HuggingFace Transformers, and provides a UI for run comparison, metric visualisation, and model registry. It requires no external account or API key. Weights & Biases is a strong alternative but involves cloud data transfer — requires explicit data governance sign-off if PI documents or their derivatives are logged.

### 6.2 What to Log Per Run

Every experiment run must log the following to MLflow:

**Parameters:**
- Model name and HuggingFace model ID
- Random seed(s)
- Learning rate, batch size, max sequence length, number of epochs
- Loss function and class weights
- Any augmentation strategy applied
- Data version hash (MD5 of train/val/test file contents)
- Pre-processing pipeline version

**Metrics (logged per epoch + final):**
- Validation macro-F1, weighted-F1, per-class F1
- Validation macro ROC-AUC, micro ROC-AUC
- Validation loss
- Training loss
- Final test metrics (logged once, at Stage 5 only)

**Artefacts:**
- Model checkpoint (best validation macro-F1 epoch)
- Classification report (JSON)
- Confusion matrix (PNG)
- Calibration plot (PNG)
- SHAP summary plot (PNG)
- `requirements.txt` / `conda env export` snapshot
- Experiment configuration YAML

### 6.3 Reproducibility Requirements

- Fix all random seeds: `random.seed(SEED)`, `np.random.seed(SEED)`, `torch.manual_seed(SEED)`, `torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(SEED)`, set `PYTHONHASHSEED=SEED` as environment variable
- Pin all library versions in `requirements.txt`; use a Docker container for full environment reproducibility
- Log the Git commit hash of the training script with every run
- Store the exact train/val/test file MD5 checksums in the run parameters
- Save the tokeniser configuration alongside the model checkpoint

### 6.4 Experiment Naming Convention

```
{model_family}_{model_variant}_{loss}_{augmentation}_{seed}_{date}

Examples:
  bert_bioclinical_ce_none_seed42_20260615
  bert_modernbert_focal_backtrans_seed7_20260616
  classical_tfidf-svm_ce_none_seed0_20260614
  llm_llama31-8b_sft_none_seed42_20260617
```

All run names are logged as MLflow tags. Runs belonging to the same model family are grouped under an MLflow experiment named `PI_Classification_{stage}` (e.g., `PI_Classification_Stage3`).

### 6.5 Model Registry

- **Staging:** After Stage 3, register the best model in the MLflow Model Registry under `PI_Classifier_Staging`
- **Production candidate:** After Stage 5 (final test evaluation), promote to `PI_Classifier_Production_Candidate`
- **Versioning:** Use semantic versioning: `v1.0.0` = first production-ready model; `v1.1.0` = same architecture, re-trained on updated data; `v2.0.0` = new model family
- **Model card:** Each registered model must be accompanied by a model card documenting: training data date range, class distribution, all evaluation metrics, known failure modes, intended use, and confidence threshold values

---

## 7. Risk Mitigation

### 7.1 Overfitting

**Risk description:** Fine-tuning large pre-trained models (110M+ parameters) on a limited labelled pharmacy dataset creates a high risk of overfitting, particularly for minority classes with few examples. Overfit models appear to perform well on the training set and validation set (if validation is inadvertently used for selection too many times) but fail to generalise to new PI documents in deployment.

**Severity:** High. Overfitting in this setting could result in systematic misclassification of rare but clinically important intervention types (e.g., contraindications).

**Mitigations:**

- **Gradual unfreezing:** During fine-tuning, freeze all BERT layers for epoch 1 (train classifier head only), then unfreeze the top 2 transformer blocks for epoch 2, then unfreeze all layers from epoch 3 onwards. This prevents catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained representations.
- **Dropout:** Apply dropout (p=0.2–0.3) on the classification head and on the pooled `[CLS]` output.
- **Weight decay:** Use AdamW with weight_decay=0.01 as L2 regularisation on all non-bias, non-LayerNorm parameters.
- **Early stopping:** Monitor validation macro-F1; stop if no improvement for 3 consecutive epochs. Do not early-stop on training loss.
- **LoRA fine-tuning:** Prefer LoRA adapters over full fine-tuning for low-data regimes. LoRA reduces trainable parameters by 95%+, dramatically reducing overfitting risk.
- **Learning rate warm-up:** Use a linear warm-up schedule for the first 10% of training steps, followed by linear decay. Prevents gradient instability in early epochs.
- **Multi-seed evaluation:** Train each model with 3 seeds; high variance across seeds indicates overfitting or training instability.

---

### 7.2 Data Leakage

**Risk description:** Pharmacy intervention records are longitudinal clinical documents. The same patient may appear dozens of times across the dataset. If patient records are split randomly at the document level, the model learns patient-specific linguistic patterns (e.g., pharmacist notes about a specific patient's known allergies) rather than generalisable intervention classification signals. This inflates validation metrics by 10–20+ points relative to real-world deployment performance.

**Severity:** High. Patient-level leakage is the single most common cause of over-optimistic NLP model evaluation in clinical settings.

**Mitigations:**

- **Patient-level splitting (mandatory):** If patient identifiers are available in the dataset, verify that the existing train/val/test split was performed at the patient level, not the document level. If it was not, this must be corrected before any model training.
- **Temporal holdout verification:** Confirm that the test set contains more recent PI records than the training set. If a temporal split was not used, document this as a limitation and note the risk of overestimated deployment performance.
- **Annotator de-identification:** Strip pharmacist identifiers, ward codes, and any metadata that could serve as a proxy for class label before training.
- **Post-hoc leakage audit:** After Stage 3, inspect the top SHAP features for any tokens that appear to be patient identifiers, pharmacist signatures, or department codes rather than clinical content. Remove and retrain if found.
- **Pre-processing leakage check:** Audit the data pipeline to confirm that `fit()` operations (TF-IDF, normalisation) were performed exclusively on the training set.

---

### 7.3 Class Imbalance

**Risk description:** Real pharmacy intervention corpora typically exhibit severe class imbalance — common categories (dose adjustment, drug interaction check) may account for 60–70% of records while rare but clinically critical categories (e.g., contraindication identification, therapeutic duplication) may represent < 5%. Standard cross-entropy loss causes models to optimise for majority-class accuracy, systematically ignoring minority classes.

**Severity:** High for minority classes. A model that achieves 80% overall accuracy but 20% recall on the rarest class is clinically dangerous and operationally useless for that intervention type.

**Tiered mitigation strategy** (apply in order; escalate only if lower tiers are insufficient):

| Tier | Strategy | When to Apply | Implementation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 — Always** | Class-weighted cross-entropy | All experiments | `weight = N_total / (N_classes × N_class_i)` passed to `CrossEntropyLoss(weight=...)` |
| **2 — If imbalance ratio > 5:1** | Focal Loss (γ=2, α=class_weights) | Stage 4a ablation | Replaces cross-entropy; down-weights easy majority examples |
| **3 — If minority class macro-F1 < 0.60** | Oversampling via back-translation | Stage 4c ablation | Augment minority class training docs to 1.5× original size using offline NMT |
| **4 — If any class < 100 training examples** | LLM-based synthetic data generation | Stage 4d (conditional) | GPT-4 offline generation on de-identified templates; pharmacist spot-check required |
| **5 — Deployment** | Per-class confidence threshold adjustment | After calibration | Set lower confidence thresholds for rare classes to improve recall at cost of precision |

**Evaluation:** Always report per-class precision, recall, and F1. Never use accuracy as a primary metric. Use macro-F1 (equal weight per class) as the primary selection metric.

**SMOTE caveat:** Do not apply standard SMOTE to raw TF-IDF vectors or tokenised text. Karayiğit et al. (2025) confirm SMOTE benefits are highly task-dependent for text. If interpolation-based augmentation is desired, operate in the BioClinicalBERT embedding space, not the raw feature space.

---

### 7.4 Distribution Shift & Concept Drift

**Risk description:** Drug formularies, clinical guidelines, prescribing patterns, and pharmacist documentation practices change over time. A model trained on PI records from 2022–2024 may underperform on 2026 records as new drugs enter the formulary, intervention categories evolve, or documentation templates change. This is a slow but near-certain source of production performance degradation.

**Severity:** Medium-High for long-term deployment. Expected performance degradation: 3–8 macro-F1 points per year without re-training.

**Mitigations:**

- **Temporal test set:** Ensure the test set contains the most recent records. Monitor the gap in performance between an earlier temporal validation set and the most recent test set as an indicator of drift velocity.
- **Monthly performance monitoring:** Once deployed, log predictions and periodically collect ground-truth labels from pharmacist corrections. Compute rolling macro-F1 on confirmed cases monthly.
- **Drift detection:** Implement a statistical process control chart (e.g., CUSUM or Page-Hinkley test) on the rolling macro-F1. Trigger re-training if performance drops more than 3 macro-F1 points below the deployment baseline.
- **Scheduled re-training:** Re-train the model quarterly on an expanded training set that includes recent PI records. Use LoRA adapters for efficient incremental adaptation.
- **New class monitoring:** Monitor prediction confidence distributions for signs of a new intervention type not represented in the training set (systematic low-confidence predictions may indicate an out-of-distribution class).

---

### 7.5 PHI / HIPAA Compliance

**Risk description:** Pharmacy intervention documents contain protected health information (PHI): patient names, MRNs, diagnoses, medication histories, and pharmacist-authored clinical notes. Any use of external APIs or cloud services with this data without appropriate safeguards constitutes a potential HIPAA violation.

**Severity:** Critical. A PHI breach is both a regulatory and patient safety event.

**Mitigations by model tier:**

| Model Tier | PHI Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Classical ML, BERT-class (self-hosted) | None | All processing on-premise; no external data transfer |
| Fine-tuned small LLM (self-hosted) | None | Deploy on institution-owned GPU infrastructure |
| GPT-4 / Claude API (commercial) | **HIGH** | Only permissible with a signed BAA and use of a HIPAA-eligible API tier. Consult data governance before any use. Prefer de-identified documents or synthetic templates if API use is required. |
| MLflow tracking server | Low | Self-host on-premise. Do not log raw document text as artefacts — log only metrics, parameters, and model weights. |

**General rules:**
- Never commit PI documents or any dataset containing PHI to a code repository (Git or otherwise)
- Encrypt all data at rest and in transit within the training pipeline
- Implement role-based access control on the model training environment and the MLflow server
- Obtain IRB/ethics approval and data governance sign-off before training on patient-identifiable pharmacy records

---

## Appendix: Evaluation Metrics Reference

All experiments report the following metrics. **Stakeholder-mandated metrics** are marked with ★.

| Metric | Description | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| ★ **Macro ROC-AUC** | Mean one-vs-rest AUC across all 5 classes; equal weight per class | **Mandatory — stakeholder** |
| ★ **Micro ROC-AUC** | Aggregate ROC-AUC weighted by class frequency | **Mandatory — stakeholder** |
| ★ **Max F1** | Highest F1 achievable across all possible thresholds per class; reported as macro-averaged | **Mandatory — stakeholder** |
| **Macro-F1** | Mean F1 across all 5 classes; equal weight; primary model selection metric | Primary selection metric |
| **Weighted-F1** | F1 weighted by class support; reflects overall system performance | Secondary |
| **Per-class P/R/F1** | Individual precision, recall, F1 for each of the 5 intervention classes | Always report |
| **Confusion Matrix** | 5×5 matrix of predictions vs ground truth; reveals systematic confusions | Always report |
| **Cohen's κ** | Agreement beyond chance; required for clinical validation publications | Clinical validation |
| **ECE** | Expected Calibration Error; measures how well confidence scores reflect true probabilities | Deployment |
| **Balanced Accuracy** | Mean recall across all classes; robust complement to macro-F1 | Supplement |

> **Do not use raw accuracy as a primary or selection metric.** Under class imbalance, accuracy is misleading and will favour majority-class models.
7.evaluation_metrics: macro ROC-AUC, micro ROC-AUC, max F1
